title: The Casual Oblivion of Francis Keene
author: M. Patrick Furry
description: Three generations. Four thousand miles. One spectacular final hustle. A road-trip novel about the terrible grace of finally seeing your family clearly.
shelf_talk: Funny, razor-sharp, and devastatingly tender. The ending will sit with you for weeks.

title: Play.
author: M. Patrick Furry
description: A psychological horror novel about a 24-hour VHS rental store that shouldn't exist, the insomniac it cures, and the price of every tape.
shelf_talk: For readers of Grady Hendrix and Paul Tremblay. A slow-burn that earns its dread.

title: The Lab.
author: N°1 · Annual 2026
description: Our inaugural annual publication of short fiction and essays. Currently reading submissions through January 31.
shelf_talk: We read every story. We respond to every writer. We pay on acceptance.

title: Kin
author: Tayari Jones
description: A lush historical novel about sisterhood — Jones at the peak of her powers, weaving together three generations of Black women whose fates are bound by love, sacrifice, and the long reach of history.
shelf_talk: Jones writes with the authority of someone who has earned every sentence.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: This Is Where the Serpent Lives
author: Daniyal Mueenuddin
description: A sweeping family epic set across continents and decades — Mueenuddin's long-awaited debut novel delivers on every promise his stories made.
shelf_talk: Mueenuddin renders the textures of privilege and its discontents with devastating clarity.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: Transcription
author: Ben Lerner
description: A tender meditation on technology, memory, and the gaps between what we mean and what we say — Lerner's most accessible and affecting novel yet.
shelf_talk: Lerner at his most humane — a quiet marvel.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: The Keeper
author: Tana French
description: French's latest standalone draws her Dublin Murder Squad universe into sharper moral focus — a tightly wound crime novel that refuses easy answers.
shelf_talk: French remains unmatched at building dread from the inside out.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: Star Shipped
author: Cat Sebastian
description: A witty, warm-hearted romance with serious stakes — Sebastian continues her streak of writing love stories that feel genuinely earned.
shelf_talk: Sebastian's prose is a pleasure — sharp and generous in equal measure.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: Yesteryear
author: Caro Claire Burke
description: A tradwife is flung back to the mid-19th century — Burke's debut is a darkly funny interrogation of nostalgia, gender, and the histories we think we want.
shelf_talk: Smart, surprising, and often genuinely hilarious. A debut that announces a major voice.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: Strangers: A Memoir of Marriage
author: Belle Burden
description: The intimate story of an imploding marriage — Burden writes about love and its dissolution with terrifying honesty and structural precision.
shelf_talk: One of the most searching memoirs about marriage in years.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: Days of Love and Rage
author: Anand Gopal
description: A deeply human account of a revolution — Gopal embeds with ordinary people forging extraordinary change, and the result is essential journalism at its most literary.
shelf_talk: Gopal's reporting transcends the form. This is the kind of book that changes how you see the world.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: London Falling
author: Patrick Radden Keefe
description: A mysterious death in a gilded city, and a family's search for truth — Keefe applies his trademark rigor to a story equal parts social history and detective thriller.
shelf_talk: Keefe is the best narrative nonfiction writer working today. This book proves it again.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review

title: On Morrison
author: Namwali Serpell
description: Serpell's critical study of Toni Morrison is an act of intellectual love — brilliant, rigorous, and alive to every register of Morrison's genius.
shelf_talk: Essential for anyone who loves Morrison, and persuasive for anyone who doesn't yet.
shelf_talk_by: The New York Times Book Review
